inputs.story: A group of long-time skydivers are planning a new jump today. Match each jumper to their suit color, and determine what year each began skydiving and how many total jumps each has under his belt. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
inputs.constraints:
1. Jorge is either the skydiver who wears the red suit or the jumper who started in 1982.
2. The skydiver who wears the pink suit started skydiving 1 year before Otis.
3. The jumper who wears the yellow suit started skydiving sometime after Philip.
4. The jumper who wears the lime green suit started skydiving sometime after the jumper who wears the yellow suit.
5. The jumper who wears the pink suit started skydiving 2 years before Shaun.
inputs.constants:
names: Jorge; Otis; Philip; Shaun.
years: 1982; 1983; 1984; 1985.
suit colors: lime green; pink; red; yellow.
inputs.solution:
shaun	1984	lime green
philip	1982	pink
jorge	1985	red
otis	1983	yellow

asp_generation.prompt:
<task>
You are given a logic grid puzzle with a story, numbered clues, and categorized constants. Your task is to generate a complete ASP (Answer Set Programming) program that, when solved with Clingo, yields a unique answer set representing the puzzle's solution.

Provided below are:
- two examples: `example_1` and `example_2`,
- the problem you must solve: `problem_to_solve`,
- important rules: `rules`.
</task>

<example_1>
"Against the Grain" offers hand-made wooden furniture at reasonable prices. Each item is made by an in-house employee. Using only the clues that follow, match each item to the employee who crafted it, and determine its price and the type of wood used to make it. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.

Constants:
Employees: Bonita; Yvette; Tabitha.
Prices: $225; $275; $325.
Wood types: ash; poplar; sandalwood.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
employee("Bonita"; "Yvette"; "Tabitha").
price(225; 275; 325).
wood_type("ash"; "poplar"; "sandalwood").

% Search space: for each employee E, assign exactly one price P and wood type W.
{match(E, P, W): price(P), wood_type(W)}=1 :- employee(E).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{E1=E2; P1=P2; W1=W2}=0 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), (E1,P1,W1)!=(E2,P2,W2).

% 1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
P=325 :- match(E,P,W), E="Bonita".

% 2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
P1>P2 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), W1="poplar", E2="Yvette".

% 3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
P1=P2-50 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), E1="Tabitha", W2="sandalwood".

% 4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.
{W="ash"; E="Yvette"}=1 :- match(E,P,W), P=275.

#show match/3.
```
</example_1>

<example_2>
The Winter Olympics have just wrapped up in Norway. Using only the clues that follow, determine the number of gold and silver medals won by each country. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. The four teams are the team from Bolivia, the team that won 3 gold medals, the team that won 6 silver medals, and the team from Argentina.
2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams.
5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.

Constants:
Countries: Argentina; Bolivia; Grenada; Oman.
Silver medals: 2; 6; 10; 11.
Gold medals: 1; 2; 3; 4.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
country("Argentina"; "Bolivia"; "Grenada"; "Oman").
silver_medals(2; 6; 10; 11).
gold_medals(1; 2; 3; 4).

% Search space: for each country C, assign exactly one silver medal count S and gold medal count G.
{assign(C, S, G): silver_medals(S), gold_medals(G)}=1 :- country(C).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{C1=C2; S1=S2; G1=G2}=0 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), (C1,S1,G1)!=(C2,S2,G2).

% 1. The four teams are all different — Bolivia, the 3-gold team, the 6-silver team, and Argentina each refer to distinct countries.
{C="Bolivia"; G=3; S=6; C="Argentina"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G).

% 2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Oman", S2=10.

% 3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
{G=2; G=1}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), C="Oman".

% 4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams — three pairwise inequalities.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C2!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C1!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".

% 5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
{C="Argentina"; S=2}=0 :- assign(C,S,G), G=4.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Argentina", S2=2.

% 6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.
{S=6; C="Grenada"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), G=2.

#show assign/3.
```
</example_2>

Here is the problem to be solved:
<problem_to_solve>
A group of long-time skydivers are planning a new jump today. Match each jumper to their suit color, and determine what year each began skydiving and how many total jumps each has under his belt. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Jorge is either the skydiver who wears the red suit or the jumper who started in 1982.
2. The skydiver who wears the pink suit started skydiving 1 year before Otis.
3. The jumper who wears the yellow suit started skydiving sometime after Philip.
4. The jumper who wears the lime green suit started skydiving sometime after the jumper who wears the yellow suit.
5. The jumper who wears the pink suit started skydiving 2 years before Shaun.

Constants:
names: Jorge; Otis; Philip; Shaun.
years: 1982; 1983; 1984; 1985.
suit colors: lime green; pink; red; yellow.
</problem_to_solve>

Here are some important rules to follow when generating the ASP code:
<rules>
1. Constant declarations — "category(const1; const2; ...)."
   - Category names: lowercase with underscores only (e.g., wood_type, gold_medals).
   - All constants in the same category must use the same format — all integers or all quoted strings.
   - Prefer integers: strip special characters ($225 → 225), strip units while keeping the number ("3 days" → 3, "2 months" → 2), and convert verbal or ordinal numerals (once → 1, twice → 2, third → 3, September → 9).
   - Use double-quoted strings when no integer representation is possible (e.g., "Bonita", "9:30AM").

2. Predicates
   - Use the minimum number of predicates needed to relate all categories.
   - Every category must appear in at least one predicate argument; all arguments must be distinct variables.
   - Choose a name that reflects the relationship (e.g., match, assign, schedule, launch).

3. Search space — one choice rule per predicate, anchoring on one category in the body and ranging over the rest inside the braces:
     {pred(V1, V2, ...): cat2(V2), cat3(V3), ...}=1 :- cat1(V1).

4. Uniqueness — no value in any non-anchor category appears more than once:
     {V1=V2; W1=W2; ...}=0 :- pred(V1,W1,...), pred(V2,W2,...), (V1,W1,...)!=(V2,W2,...).

5. Constraint rules — encode each clue using one or more rules of these forms, using only defined constants and predicates:
     <C1>; ...; <Cm> :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.      % if all Li hold, at least one Ci must hold
     {<C1>; ...; <Cm>}=k :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.  % if all Li hold, exactly k of Ci hold
     :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.                        % this combination is forbidden

   Common patterns:
   - "Of A and B, one is C and the other is D" → three rules: A≠B, A is C or D, B is C or D.
   - "A, B and C are all different" → three pairwise inequality rules (A≠B, A≠C, B≠C).
   - "Neither A nor B is X" → {A_cond; B_cond}=0 :- ... X ... .

6. Show directives — one "#show pred/arity." per predicate.
</rules>

ASP Program:
asp_generation.thinking:
Let's analyze the problem and generate the ASP code.

Problem:
Match each jumper (Jorge, Otis, Philip, Shaun) to their suit color (lime green, pink, red, yellow), determine what year each began skydiving (1982, 1983, 1984, 1985), and how many total jumps each has under his belt. Wait, the problem description says "how many total jumps each has under his belt", but the constants provided do not include a category for "total jumps". Let me re-read carefully.
"Match each jumper to their suit color, and determine what year each began skydiving and how many total jumps each has under his belt."
But constants are:
names: Jorge; Otis; Philip; Shaun.
years: 1982; 1983; 1984; 1985.
suit colors: lime green; pink; red; yellow.
There is no "total jumps" category in the constants. This might be a typo in the problem description or it's just a distractor. I will stick to the given constants: names, years, suit colors. I'll use a predicate like `match(Name, Year, SuitColor)`.

Let's list the clues:
1. Jorge is either the skydiver who wears the red suit or the jumper who started in 1982.
   -> `{S="red"; Y=1982}=1 :- match("Jorge", Y, S).`

2. The skydiver who wears the pink suit started skydiving 1 year before Otis.
   -> `Y_Pink = Y_Otis - 1 :- match(E1, Y1, S1), match(E2, Y2, S2), S1="pink", E2="Otis".`
   Wait, Otis is a name. So `match("Otis", Y_Otis, S_Otis)`.
   `match(E_Pink, Y_Pink, "pink")`.
   So: `Y_Pink = Y_Otis - 1 :- match(E_Pink, Y_Pink, "pink"), match("Otis", Y_Otis, _).`

3. The jumper who wears the yellow suit started skydiving sometime after Philip.
   -> `Y_Yellow > Y_Philip :- match(E_Yellow, Y_Yellow, "yellow"), match("Philip", Y_Philip, _).`

4. The jumper who wears the lime green suit started skydiving sometime after the jumper who wears the yellow suit.
   -> `Y_Lime > Y_Yellow :- match(E_Lime, Y_Lime, "lime green"), match(E_Yellow, Y_Yellow, "yellow").`

5. The jumper who wears the pink suit started skydiving 2 years before Shaun.
   -> `Y_Pink = Y_Shaun - 2 :- match(E_Pink, Y_Pink, "pink"), match("Shaun", Y_Shaun, _).`

Constants:
names: "Jorge"; "Otis"; "Philip"; "Shaun"
years: 1982; 1983; 1984; 1985
suit colors: "lime green"; "pink"; "red"; "yellow"

Let's check the years for clue 2 and 5:
Pink is 1 year before Otis.
Pink is 2 years before Shaun.
So Otis is 1 year after Pink, Shaun is 2 years after Pink.
Thus, Shaun = Otis + 1.
Years available: 1982, 1983, 1984, 1985.
Possible assignments for (Pink, Otis, Shaun):
If Pink=1982, Otis=1983, Shaun=1984. (Fits)
If Pink=1983, Otis=1984, Shaun=1985. (Fits)
If Pink=1984, Otis=1985, Shaun=1986 (out of range).
So Pink is either 1982 or 1983.

Let's encode these carefully.

Search space:
`{match(N, Y, S): years(Y), suit_color(S)}=1 :- name(N).`

Uniqueness:
`{N1=N2; Y1=Y2; S1=S2}=0 :- match(N1,Y1,S1), match(N2,Y2,S2), (N1,Y1,S1)!=(N2,Y2,S2).`

Clues:
1. `{S="red"; Y=1982}=1 :- match("Jorge", Y, S).`
2. `Y_P = Y_O - 1 :- match(E_P, Y_P, "pink"), match("Otis", Y_O, _).`
3. `Y_Y > Y_Philip :- match(E_Y, Y_Y, "yellow"), match("Philip", Y_Philip, _).`
4. `Y_L > Y_Y :- match(E_L, Y_L, "lime green"), match(E_Y, Y_Y, "yellow").`
5. `Y_P = Y_S - 2 :- match(E_P, Y_P, "pink"), match("Shaun", Y_S, _).`

Wait, clue 3 says "sometime after", which means strictly greater.
Clue 4 says "sometime after", strictly greater.
Clue 2 and 5 are exact differences.

Let's verify the constraints with Clingo syntax.
`Y_P = Y_O - 1 :- match(E_P, Y_P, "pink"), match("Otis", Y_O, _).`
`Y_Y > Y_Philip :- match(E_Y, Y_Y, "yellow"), match("Philip", Y_Philip, _).`
`Y_L > Y_Y :- match(E_L, Y_L, "lime green"), match(E_Y, Y_Y, "yellow").`
`Y_P = Y_S - 2 :- match(E_P, Y_P, "pink"), match("Shaun", Y_S, _).`

Is there any issue with using variables in the head? Yes, in ASP, we can use arithmetic in the head, but it's better to express it as a constraint or use a rule that binds them. Actually, `Y_P = Y_O - 1 :- ...` is valid in Clingo. Alternatively, we can write `:- match(E_P, Y_P, "pink"), match("Otis", Y_O, _), Y_P != Y_O - 1.` But the equality head is fine.

Let's stick to the example format for arithmetic:
`Y1=Y2-1 :- match(E1,Y1,"pink"), match("Otis",Y2,_).`
`Y1>Y2 :- match(E1,Y1,"yellow"), match("Philip",Y2,_).`
`Y1>Y2 :- match(E1,Y1,"lime green"), match(E2,Y2,"yellow").`
`Y1=Y2-2 :- match(E1,Y1,"pink"), match("Shaun",Y2,_).`

So `Y_pink = Y_Otis - 1`.
In clue 5: "The jumper who wears the pink suit started skydiving 2 years before Shaun."
So `Y_pink = Y_Shaun - 2`.

These two imply `Y_Otis = Y_Shaun - 1`.
And `Y_pink` is either 1982 or 1983.

Let's assemble the ASP program.

year(1982; 1983; 1984; 1985).
suit_color("lime green"; "pink"; "red"; "yellow").

Search space:
{match(N, Y, S): year(Y), suit_color(S)}=1 :- name(N).

Uniqueness:
{N1=N2; Y1=Y2; S1=S2}=0 :- match(N1,Y1,S1), match(N2,Y2,S2), (N1,Y1,S1)!=(N2,Y2,S2).

Clues:
1. {S="red"; Y=1982}=1 :- match("Jorge", Y, S).
2. Y1=Y2-1 :- match(E1,Y1,"pink"), match("Otis",Y2,_).
3. Y1>Y2 :- match(E1,Y1,"yellow"), match("Philip",Y2,_).
4. Y1>Y2 :- match(E1,Y1,"lime green"), match(E2,Y2,"yellow").
5. Y1=Y2-2 :- match(E1,Y1,"pink"), match("Shaun",Y2,_).

Wait, clue 4 says "lime green suit started skydiving sometime after the jumper who wears the yellow suit."
So `Y_lime > Y_yellow`.

Check if all clues are correctly translated.
Clue 1: "Jorge is either the skydiver who wears the red suit or the jumper who started in 1982." -> `{S="red"; Y=1982}=1 :- match("Jorge", Y, S).` Correct.
Clue 2: "The skydiver who wears the pink suit started skydiving 1 year before Otis." -> `Y1=Y2-1 :- match(E1,Y1,"pink"), match("Otis",Y2,_).` Correct.
Clue 3: "The jumper who wears the yellow suit started skydiving sometime after Philip." -> `Y1>Y2 :- match(E1,Y1,"yellow"), match("Philip",Y2,_).` Correct.

Clue 5: "The jumper who wears the pink suit started skydiving 2 years before Shaun." -> `Y1=Y2-2 :- match(E1,Y1,"pink"), match("Shaun",Y2,_).` Correct.

Let's test mentally if this yields a unique solution.
From 2 and 5: `Y_pink = Y_Otis - 1` and `Y_pink = Y_Shaun - 2`.
So `Y_Otis = Y_pink + 1`, `Y_Shaun = Y_pink + 2`.

Case 1: `Y_pink = 1982`. Then `Y_Otis = 1983`, `Y_Shaun = 1984`. Remaining year for Philip is 1985.
Case 2: `Y_pink = 1983`. Then `Y_Otis = 1984`, `Y_Shaun = 1985`. Remaining year for Philip is 1982.

In Case 1: `Y_Philip = 1985`. No year is > 1985. So Case 1 is invalid.
Thus, Case 2 must be true: `Y_pink = 1983`, `Y_Otis = 1984`, `Y_Shaun = 1985`, `Y_Philip = 1982`.
Check clue 3: `Y_yellow > 1982`. So `Y_yellow` can be 1983, 1984, or 1985.
But `Y_pink = 1983`, so yellow cannot be 1983.
So `Y_yellow` is either 1984 or 1985.
Check clue 4: `Y_lime > Y_yellow`.
If `Y_yellow = 1984`, then `Y_lime` must be 1985. But 1985 is taken by Shaun (who has pink suit). So `Y_lime` cannot be 1985 because suits must be unique. Wait, `Y_lime` is the year for the lime green suit. Each person has a unique suit and unique year.

Otis: 1984
Shaun: 1985
Philip: 1982

We have 4 names. Years are 1982, 1983, 1984, 1985.
We know:
Otis: 1984
Shaun: 1985

So Jorge must be 1983.
Thus, Jorge wears pink. (Since `Y_pink = 1983`)

Maybe `Y_pink` is not necessarily worn by one of the named people? No, "The skydiver who wears the pink suit" refers to one of the four.

Ah, `Y_pink = Y_Otis - 1` and `Y_pink = Y_Shaun - 2`.

1. Jorge is Red or 1982. -> Jorge is Red. (True)
2. Pink(Philip, 1982) started 1 year before Otis(1983). -> 1982 = 1983 - 1. (True)

4. Lime(Shaun, 1984) started after Yellow(Otis, 1983). -> 1984 > 1983. (True)
5. Pink(Philip, 1982) started 2 years before Shaun(1984). -> 1982 = 1984 - 2. (True)

This works perfectly! My earlier deduction that Pink must be Jorge was wrong because I assumed Pink was a year, not a suit assigned to a name. Shaun: Lime Green, 1984

Everything is consistent.

Now, let's write out the ASP code carefully.

Constants:

Search space:

```asp
% Constants
name("Jorge"; "Otis"; "Philip"; "Shaun").
year(1982; 1983; 1984; 1985).
suit_color("lime green"; "pink"; "red"; "yellow").

% Search space: for each name N, assign exactly one year Y and suit color S.
{match(N, Y, S): year(Y), suit_color(S)}=1 :- name(N).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{N1=N2; Y1=Y2; S1=S2}=0 :- match(N1,Y1,S1), match(N2,Y2,S2), (N1,Y1,S1)!=(N2,Y2,S2).

% 1. Jorge is either the skydiver who wears the red suit or the jumper who started in 1982.
{S="red"; Y=1982}=1 :- match("Jorge", Y, S).

% 2. The skydiver who wears the pink suit started skydiving 1 year before Otis.
Y1=Y2-1 :- match(E1,Y1,"pink"), match("Otis",Y2,_).

% 3. The jumper who wears the yellow suit started skydiving sometime after Philip.
Y1>Y2 :- match(E1,Y1,"yellow"), match("Philip",Y2,_).

% 4. The jumper who wears the lime green suit started skydiving sometime after the jumper who wears the yellow suit.
Y1>Y2 :- match(E1,Y1,"lime green"), match(E2,Y2,"yellow").

% 5. The jumper who wears the pink suit started skydiving 2 years before Shaun.
Y1=Y2-2 :- match(E1,Y1,"pink"), match("Shaun",Y2,_).

#show match/3.
```
